message.content:
Repo: /Users/thomasjones/hc-assay (branch harden/pass-1). Venv (run PoCs): /Users/thomasjones/hc-assay/.venv/bin/python.
This is a dataset-agnostic ML/methodology engine being hardened for PRODUCTION. Hard constraints (do NOT flag as bugs): local-first/data-sovereign (loopback only, nothing leaves the box), no metered API (subscription OAuth only), engine never imports adapters/datasets (ADR-0002), optional heavy backends are lazy-imported (ADR-0006). Read real code; prefer findings you can back with a concrete repro/PoC against the venv. Report ONLY genuine production-readiness defects (not style/nits). Each finding needs a real file path and, where possible, a line.

You are independent VERIFIER #1 of 2. A prior adversarial assessor reported this production-readiness finding:

TITLE: corpus_fingerprint is recomputed redundantly per study (ingest + every build_baseline_artifact call), each a full canonical copy + sort + JSON of the whole corpus
DIMENSION: performance & scale
CLAIMED SEVERITY: MEDIUM
FILE: src/assay_engine/baseline/determinism.py 44-55 (corpus_fingerprint); 104 (build_baseline_artifact recomputes); pipeline.py:280
DESCRIPTION: corpus_fingerprint builds a full list-comprehension copy of every unit and relation, sorts all units by id, canonicalizes every attribute mapping via _plain, and json.dumps the whole thing — O(total corpus bytes) time and memory. run_study computes it once at ingest (pipeline.py:280, `cfp`). Then build_baseline_artifact (determinism.py:104) computes it again from scratch for the determinism record's `input_hashes['corpus']` and `corpus_fingerprint`; a study's BaselineBuilder that calls build_baseline_artifact thus repeats the full-corpus canonicalization a second (and the builder may itself fingerprint, a third) time per run. PoC against the venv: 50k text units = 0.43 s and 47 MB per call. Breaking point: at ~500k-1M units each call is ~5-10 s and ~0.5 GB, paid 2-3x per run with no caching. The fingerprint is purely a function of corpus content and could be computed once and threaded through.
SUGGESTED FIX: Compute corpus_fingerprint once and pass it into build_baseline_artifact (e.g. an optional `corpus_hash` arg) instead of recomputing; or memoize on the Corpus instance (it is frozen/immutable). Threading the already-computed `cfp` from run_study into the determinism harness removes the duplicate full-corpus pass entirely.

Independently determine whether this is a TRUE production-readiness problem. Read the actual code; attempt a concrete PoC against the venv if applicable. Set is_real=false if it is a false positive, already-handled, or merely a documented/justified design choice given the hard constraints. Recalibrate severity. Provide your evidence (what you read/ran).

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.content:
1	"""Determinism & reproducibility harness for the baseline (ADR-0001, METHODOLOGY.md §1, §7).
2	
3	A baseline is only a yardstick if the *same inputs reproduce the same baseline*. This module
4	makes that contract concrete and engine-level: it content-hashes inputs, derives seeds
5	deterministically from those hashes, records component versions, and stamps all of it into a
6	:class:`~assay_engine.baseline.toolkit.BaselineArtifact`'s ``determinism`` record — so a
7	hostile reviewer can confirm a baseline was produced exactly as claimed.
8	
9	It is dependency-free and pure. The heavy, *choice-bearing* builders (which embedding model,
10	which clustering algorithm, which graph construction) encode dataset/study decisions and are
11	supplied by a study's adapter as :class:`~assay_engine.baseline.toolkit.BaselineBuilder`
12	implementations — not prescribed by the engine (ADR-0002). This harness records *how* such a
13	builder ran so the result is reproducible regardless of which builder it was.
14	"""
15	
16	from __future__ import annotations
17	
18	import hashlib
19	import json
20	import sys
21	from dataclasses import dataclass
22	from typing import Any, Mapping
23	
24	from assay_engine import __version__ as _ENGINE_VERSION
25	
26	# The canonical hashing primitives moved to ``assay_engine._canonical`` (see note below); these
27	# imports re-export them at their historical home so existing call sites keep working.
28	from assay_engine._canonical import canonical_plain as _plain  # noqa: F401
29	from assay_engine._canonical import hash_bytes as hash_bytes  # noqa: F401  (re-export)
30	from assay_engine._canonical import hash_text
31	from assay_engine._canonical import hash_value as hash_value  # re-export (baseline public API)
32	from assay_engine._canonical import keytag as _keytag  # noqa: F401
33	from assay_engine._frozen import freeze_mapping
34	from assay_engine.baseline.toolkit import BaselineArtifact
35	from assay_engine.contracts.schema import Corpus
36	
37	# The canonical hashing primitives (``_plain``/``_keytag``/``hash_bytes``/``hash_text``/
38	# ``hash_value``) live in ``assay_engine._canonical`` so they sit below both ``baseline`` and
39	# ``methodology`` in the import graph (audit #B1/#B2/#B5 originally landed here; hoisted so
40	# pre-registration can content-bind a hypothesis with the *same* type-faithful digest). They are
41	# re-exported above for back-compat.
42	
43	
44	def corpus_fingerprint(corpus: Corpus) -> str:
45	    """Deterministic content hash of a corpus (order-independent across units/relations)."""
46	    units = [
47	        {"id": u.unit_id, "text": u.text, "attrs": _plain(u.attributes)}
48	        for u in sorted(corpus.units, key=lambda u: u.unit_id)
49	    ]
50	    relations = [
51	        {"s": r.source_id, "t": r.target_id, "k": r.kind, "attrs": _plain(r.attributes)}
52	        for r in sorted(corpus.relations, key=lambda r: (r.source_id, r.target_id, r.kind))
53	    ]
54	    payload = {"units": units, "relations": relations, "metadata": _plain(corpus.metadata)}
55	    return hash_text(json.dumps(payload, sort_keys=True, allow_nan=False))
56	
57	
58	def stable_seed(*parts: str, bits: int = 32) -> int:
59	    """A deterministic non-negative integer seed derived from ``parts``.
60	
61	    Derived from a hash so the same inputs always yield the same seed — reproducible without a
62	    hard-coded magic number, and distinct inputs get distinct seeds.
63	    """
64	    digest = hashlib.sha256("\x1f".join(parts).encode("utf-8")).hexdigest()
65	    return int(digest, 16) % (1 << bits)
66	
67	
68	@dataclass(frozen=True, slots=True)
69	class DeterminismRecord:
70	    """The reproducibility provenance stamped onto a baseline (ADR-0001)."""
71	
72	    seed: int
73	    input_hashes: Mapping[str, str]
74	    component_versions: Mapping[str, str]
75	
76	    def __post_init__(self) -> None:
77	        object.__setattr__(self, "input_hashes", freeze_mapping(self.input_hashes))
78	        object.__setattr__(self, "component_versions", freeze_mapping(self.component_versions))
79	
80	    def as_dict(self) -> dict[str, Any]:
81	        return {
82	            "seed": self.seed,
83	            "input_hashes": dict(self.input_hashes),
84	            "component_versions": dict(self.component_versions),
85	        }
86	
87	
88	def build_baseline_artifact(
89	    corpus: Corpus,
90	    contents: Mapping[str, Any],
91	    *,
92	    component_versions: Mapping[str, str] | None = None,
93	    extra_inputs: Mapping[str, Any] | None = None,
94	    seed: int | None = None,
95	) -> BaselineArtifact:
96	    """Assemble a :class:`BaselineArtifact` with a complete determinism record.
97	
98	    ``contents`` is the builder's output (embeddings, similarity graph, clusters, …).
99	    ``component_versions`` records the builder + any model/library versions (the engine and
100	    Python versions are added automatically). ``extra_inputs`` are additional inputs to hash
101	    (e.g. config). ``seed``, if omitted, is derived deterministically from the corpus + inputs
102	    so it is reproducible.
103	    """
104	    corpus_hash = corpus_fingerprint(corpus)
105	    if extra_inputs and "corpus" in extra_inputs:
106	        raise ValueError("'corpus' is a reserved input-hash key — rename the extra input (#B7)")
107	    input_hashes = {"corpus": corpus_hash}
108	    for name, value in (extra_inputs or {}).items():
109	        input_hashes[name] = hash_value(value)
110	
111	    versions = {"engine": _ENGINE_VERSION, "python": sys.version.split()[0]}
112	    versions.update(component_versions or {})
113	
114	    if seed is None:
115	        # Derive from all input hashes (the corpus hash is already among them under "corpus").
116	        seed = stable_seed(*(input_hashes[k] for k in sorted(input_hashes)))
117	
118	    record = DeterminismRecord(seed=seed, input_hashes=input_hashes, component_versions=versions)
119	    return BaselineArtifact(
120	        corpus_fingerprint=corpus_hash, contents=contents, determinism=record.as_dict()
121	    )
122

content.text:
Now I understand the full picture. Let me confirm the data flow. The key question: is `build_baseline_artifact` actually called in the `run_study` path (the `baseline_builder.build` at line 336), causing the recomputation the finding claims?

The builder is supplied by the study/adapter and *may* call `build_baseline_artifact`. Line 337 then re-verifies `baseline.corpus_fingerprint != cfp` — but that's just a string compare, no recompute. Let me check the Corpus schema (frozen/immutable?) and run a PoC.

content.content:
27:from assay_engine._frozen import freeze_mapping
30:@dataclass(frozen=True, slots=True)
31:class Unit:
48:@dataclass(frozen=True, slots=True)
49:class Relation:
66:@dataclass(frozen=True, slots=True)
67:class Corpus:
74:    def unit_ids(self) -> frozenset[str]:
75:        return frozenset(u.unit_id for u in self.units)

66	@dataclass(frozen=True, slots=True)
67	class Corpus:
68	    """The full canonical dataset handed to the baseline builder."""
69	
70	    units: tuple[Unit, ...]
71	    relations: tuple[Relation, ...] = ()
72	    metadata: Mapping[str, Any] = field(default_factory=dict)
73	
74	    def unit_ids(self) -> frozenset[str]:
75	        return frozenset(u.unit_id for u in self.units)
76	
77	    def __post_init__(self) -> None:
78	        ids = [u.unit_id for u in self.units]
79	        if len(ids) != len(set(ids)):
80	            raise ValueError("Corpus contains duplicate unit_id values")
81	        # A closed canonical corpus must not contain relations to non-existent units
82	        # (audit pass 1, issue #3) — mirrors the duplicate-id and FeatureMatrix checks.
83	        known = set(ids)
84	        dangling = sorted(
85	            {e for r in self.relations for e in (r.source_id, r.target_id) if e not in known}
86	        )
87	        if dangling:
88	            raise ValueError(f"Corpus has relations referencing unknown unit_id(s): {dangling[:5]}")
89	        object.__setattr__(self, "metadata", freeze_mapping(self.metadata))
90

content.text: Corpus is `frozen=True, slots=True`. With `slots=True`, `functools.cached_property` won't work (no `__dict__`), which is likely why memoization wasn't trivially added. Now let me run a PoC to measure the actual cost of `corpus_fingerprint` and confirm the recompute count.

input.command:
cd /Users/thomasjones/hc-assay && .venv/bin/python - <<'PY'
import time, tracemalloc
from assay_engine.contracts.schema import Corpus, Unit
from assay_engine.baseline import determinism as D

# Build a corpus of 50k text units
N = 50000
units = tuple(Unit(unit_id=f"u{i}", text="lorem ipsum dolor sit amet "*5, attributes={"a": i, "b": f"v{i}"}) for i in range(N))
corpus = Corpus(units=units)

# time one corpus_fingerprint
t0=time.perf_counter()
tracemalloc.start()
h = D.corpus_fingerprint(corpus)
cur, peak = tracemalloc.get_traced_memory()
tracemalloc.stop()
t1=time.perf_counter()
print(f"corpus_fingerprint: {t1-t0:.3f}s  peak_mem={peak/1e6:.1f}MB  hash={h[:12]}")

# build_baseline_artifact recompute
t0=time.perf_counter()
art = D.build_baseline_artifact(corpus, contents={"x":1})
t1=time.perf_counter()
print(f"build_baseline_artifact: {t1-t0:.3f}s  (recomputes fingerprint internally)")
print("match:", art.corpus_fingerprint == h)
PY

content.content:
corpus_fingerprint: 0.726s  peak_mem=60.3MB  hash=85f2328469a0
build_baseline_artifact: 0.152s  (recomputes fingerprint internally)
match: True

content.text:
The PoC confirms ~0.7s and ~60MB per call for 50k units (close to the claimed 0.43s/47MB; warm vs cold differences). The fingerprint of the corpus is computed at least twice per run: once at `pipeline.py:280` (`cfp`), and once inside `build_baseline_artifact` (if the study's builder uses it).

Now let me verify whether the builder in run_study actually calls `build_baseline_artifact` (the recompute the finding claims), and check the reference adapter to assess real-world incidence.

input.description: Find adapter/test usages of the fingerprint functions

content.content:

content.text:
The reference adapter (`tests/reference_study.py:53`) and `examples/minimal_study.py:54` both call `build_baseline_artifact` inside their builder's `build`. So in the canonical run_study path: fingerprint is computed at `pipeline.py:280` (cfp) AND again inside `build_baseline_artifact` during `baseline_builder.build` at line 336 — exactly the duplication claimed. That's 2x per run for the reference path.

Now let me assess severity realistically. Key context: this is a local-first engine. Let me check the scale claims and whether there's any documented justification.

content.content:
40	    def parse(self, source: Path) -> Corpus:
41	        data = json.loads(Path(source).read_text(encoding="utf-8"))
42	        return Corpus(units=tuple(Unit(u["id"], u["text"]) for u in data["units"]))
43	
44	    def source_fingerprint(self, source: Path) -> str:
45	        return hashlib.sha256(Path(source).read_bytes()).hexdigest()
46	
47	
48	class ReferenceBaselineBuilder:
49	    """Blind baseline: a deterministic summary stat over the corpus text only."""
50	
51	    def build(self, corpus: Corpus, *, claim_guard: ClaimBlindGuard[object]) -> BaselineArtifact:
52	        mean_len = sum(len(u.text) for u in corpus.units) / max(1, len(corpus.units))
53	        return build_baseline_artifact(
54	            corpus,
55	            {"mean_text_len": mean_len, "n_units": len(corpus.units)},
56	            component_versions={"builder": "reference@1"},
57	        )
58

content.text:
Confirmed. The finding is accurate: in the canonical run path the corpus fingerprint is computed twice (pipeline.py:280 + inside the builder's `build_baseline_artifact`), each a full O(corpus) canonical copy + sort + JSON dump.

Let me now calibrate severity. The PoC shows ~0.7s/60MB at 50k units. The claimed breakpoint (500k-1M units → 5-10s, 0.5GB, paid 2-3x) is a linear extrapolation. This runs once per study, not per request — it's a batch ML pipeline, not a hot loop. The cost is real but bounded and one-time per study run. The data-sovereign/local-first constraint doesn't justify wasted compute, so it's a legitimate (if modest) production-readiness inefficiency, not a "documented design choice."

Let me verify the extrapolation is plausible and that there's genuinely no caching anywhere.

input.command:
cd /Users/thomasjones/hc-assay && .venv/bin/python - <<'PY'
import time
from assay_engine.contracts.schema import Corpus, Unit
from assay_engine.baseline import determinism as D
for N in (100000, 200000):
    units = tuple(Unit(unit_id=f"u{i}", text="lorem ipsum dolor sit amet "*5) for i in range(N))
    c = Corpus(units=units)
    t0=time.perf_counter(); D.corpus_fingerprint(c); t1=time.perf_counter()
    print(f"N={N}: {t1-t0:.3f}s per fingerprint call")
PY

content.content:
N=100000: 0.179s per fingerprint call
N=200000: 0.391s per fingerprint call

content.text:
Interesting — with shared text (no per-unit attrs) it's faster and clearly linear (~0.39s at 200k → extrapolates to ~2s at 1M per call). With per-unit attributes (first PoC) it was ~0.7s at 50k. Either way the extrapolation to multi-second, sub-GB cost at 500k-1M units, paid 2x, holds directionally. It's linear in corpus size and genuinely duplicated.

Assessment complete.

**Verdict: TRUE finding, but severity MEDIUM is too high — recalibrate to LOW.**

The duplication is real and confirmed by code + PoC:
- `pipeline.py:280` computes `cfp = corpus_fingerprint(corpus)`.
- The reference/example builders call `build_baseline_artifact` (reference_study.py:53, examples/minimal_study.py:54), which recomputes the same fingerprint at `determinism.py:104`.
- Net: 2x full-corpus canonicalization per study run in the canonical path. The finding's "3x" requires a builder that *also* fingerprints separately; not present in shipped code, so it's 2x in practice.

But this is once per study run (a batch pipeline), not per-request hot path. PoC: ~0.7s/60MB at 50k units; linear, so ~2-7s/0.5GB at 1M units paid twice. That is wasted work and worth fixing (thread `cfp` through, or add a `corpus_hash` arg to `build_baseline_artifact`), but it is a modest one-time-per-run inefficiency, not a scaling wall. The "corpus" reserved-key collision guard at determinism.py:105-106 is the only complication for threading. Note `Corpus` is `frozen=True, slots=True`, so `cached_property` won't work directly (no `__dict__`) — the suggested arg-threading fix is the right approach.

input.reasoning:
The finding is technically accurate. corpus_fingerprint (src/assay_engine/baseline/determinism.py:44-55) builds a full list-comprehension copy of every unit and relation, sorts units by id, canonicalizes every attribute mapping via _plain, and json.dumps the whole payload — O(corpus) time and memory. In the canonical run_study path it is computed twice per run: once at pipeline.py:280 (cfp), and again inside build_baseline_artifact (determinism.py:104) which the study's BaselineBuilder calls during baseline_builder.build (pipeline.py:336). This double-compute is real for the shipped reference path: both tests/reference_study.py:53 and examples/minimal_study.py:54 call build_baseline_artifact inside their builder. There is no caching/memoization (Corpus is frozen+slots, so cached_property cannot trivially attach). pipeline.py:337 only string-compares the fingerprints, so it does not recompute.

However, severity MEDIUM is overstated. (1) Frequency: this is paid once per *study run* in a batch ML/methodology pipeline — not a per-request or per-item hot path. (2) The claimed 3x is not present in shipped code (would require a builder that separately fingerprints); the real factor is 2x. (3) Cost is bounded and linear; even the extrapolated worst case is a few seconds and sub-GB of transient memory, once per run. The local-first/data-sovereign constraint does not justify the waste, so it is a legitimate efficiency defect worth the trivial fix (thread the already-computed cfp into build_baseline_artifact via an optional corpus_hash arg), but it does not threaten correctness or realistic operability. LOW.

Read src/assay_engine/pipeline.py: cfp computed at line 280, baseline_builder.build at 336, fingerprint string-compare (no recompute) at 337. Confirmed Corpus is @dataclass(frozen=True, slots=True) in src/assay_engine/contracts/schema.py:66-89. grep shows shipped builders call build_baseline_artifact (tests/reference_study.py:53, examples/minimal_study.py:54), confirming the 2x path; no caching anywhere. PoC against /Users/thomasjones/hc-assay/.venv: 50k units with per-unit attrs = 0.726s, 60.3MB peak per corpus_fingerprint call; build_baseline_artifact then recomputed (match=True). Scaling PoC (shared text): N=100k 0.179s, N=200k 0.391s — linear, extrapolating to ~2s at 1M per call, paid 2x. The reserved 'corpus' key guard at determinism.py:105-106 is the only minor complication for the suggested arg-threading fix.

content.text: Verified. The finding is real but over-rated — recalibrated to LOW. Details in the StructuredOutput call above.
